lessons.title: Headings
lessons.content:
HTML gives you six heading levels: h1 through h6. h1 is the most important. h6 is the least. Each level visually decreases in size by default, but that is just the browser's stylesheet. The real meaning is hierarchy, not size.

Every page should have exactly one h1. It describes the overall topic of the page. Think of it like the title of a book. h2 elements are the chapter titles. h3 elements are section headings within those chapters. You keep nesting from there.

Do not choose heading levels based on how big you want the text to look. That is what CSS is for. Choose heading levels based on the document outline. A screen reader user navigating by headings will be confused if you skip from h1 to h4 for visual reasons.

Headings also matter for SEO. Search engines use your heading structure to understand what a page is about.

questions.q: How many h1 elements should a page have?
options: As many as you need for the content
One, representing the overall topic of the page
One per major section of content
It does not matter as long as they are styled correctly
questions.explanation: One h1 per page. It is the top of the document outline. Multiple h1s confuse screen readers and weaken your SEO signal.

questions.q: You want a subheading inside an h2 section. Which element do you use?
options: h1, because it is the most prominent
h4, to leave room for more levels later
h3, the next level down in the hierarchy
h2 again, since it is the same kind of content
questions.explanation: h3 is the correct next level inside an h2 section. You go down the hierarchy in order rather than skipping levels or repeating them.

questions.q: Why should you not pick heading levels based on desired text size?
options: Because headings cannot be restyled with CSS
Because heading levels communicate document structure, not visual size
Because browsers ignore heading size differences
Because only h1 and h2 are widely supported
questions.explanation: Heading levels define the outline of your document. Screen readers, search engines, and browser reading modes all depend on that outline being meaningful. Use CSS to change visual size.

lessons.title: Paragraphs and Inline Text
lessons.content:
The p element is the default container for a block of text. Every paragraph gets its own p element. Browsers add a margin above and below paragraphs automatically.

Inside a paragraph, you can use inline elements to add meaning to specific words or phrases. strong marks text as strongly important and renders bold by default. em marks text as emphasised and renders italic by default. These carry semantic meaning — do not use them just to make text bold or italic. Use CSS for that.

Other useful inline elements: small marks fine print or secondary information. mark highlights text like a highlighter pen. code marks inline code snippets. abbr marks abbreviations and accepts a title attribute with the expanded form.

The br element inserts a line break. It is a void element with no closing tag. Use it sparingly — for addresses or poems where line breaks are part of the content, not as a spacing hack. Use CSS margins for spacing.

examples.label: Paragraphs and inline elements
examples.code:
HTML is not a programming language. It is a description language.

The alt attribute is required on every image.

HTML was created in 1991.

Line one.
Line two.

questions.q: What is the semantic difference between strong and b?
options: There is no difference, they both make text bold
strong means the text is important, b is purely visual bold with no added meaning
b is for headings, strong is for paragraphs
strong is deprecated and b should be used instead
questions.explanation: strong communicates importance. b is a visual style with no semantic weight. Screen readers may announce strong content differently. Use strong when the content matters, b when you just want bold styling.

questions.q: When should you use the br element?
options: To add spacing between any two elements on the page
When content requires a line break as part of its meaning, like an address or poem
As an alternative to the p element for short text
Whenever you want text on a new line inside a div
questions.explanation: br is for meaningful line breaks — where the line break is part of the content itself. For spacing between elements, use CSS margin or padding.

questions.q: Which element would you use to mark up an inline piece of code in a sentence?
options: pre, because code needs a fixed-width font
em, because code needs to stand out
code, the inline element for code snippets
kbd, the keyboard input element
questions.explanation: code is the correct inline element for a code snippet in a sentence. pre is for multi-line preformatted code blocks. kbd is specifically for keyboard input.

lessons.title: Spot the Bug: Heading Hierarchy
lessons.prompt: One line breaks the heading hierarchy in a way that harms accessibility. Click it.
lessons.code:
My Cooking Blog

This Week's Recipes

Here are the recipes I made this week.

Monday: Pasta
lessons.explanation: Jumping from h1 directly to h4 skips two levels. The correct element here is h2. Screen readers and search engines expect a logical outline — h1 then h2, not h1 then h4.

lessons.title: Ordered, Unordered, and Description Lists
lessons.content:
HTML has three list types. Each one communicates something different about the relationship between items.

ul is an unordered list. Use it when the items have no meaningful sequence — a list of ingredients, a navigation menu, a set of features. Items render with bullet points by default.

ol is an ordered list. Use it when the sequence matters — steps in a recipe, instructions, rankings. Items render with numbers by default. The start attribute lets you begin at a number other than 1. The reversed attribute counts down instead of up.

dl is a description list. Use it for term-definition pairs — a glossary, metadata, key-value information. It uses dt for the term and dd for the description. One dt can have multiple dd elements and vice versa.

In all three types, li is the list item element. Lists can be nested inside each other by placing a full ul or ol inside an li element.

examples.label: All three list types
examples.code:
• Flour
  • Eggs
  • Butter
  1. Preheat the oven to 180°C
  2. Mix the dry ingredients
  3. Add the eggs
HTML
The structure layer of the web
CSS
The presentation layer of the web

questions.q: You are writing a numbered step-by-step guide. Which list element do you use?
options: ul, because lists always use bullet points
ol, because the order of steps matters
dl, because each step has a label and a description
li on its own, without a parent list element
questions.explanation: ol is for ordered lists where sequence is meaningful. Recipe steps, instructions, and ranked items all belong in an ol.

questions.q: Which elements make up a description list?
options: dl, li, and dd
dl, dt, and dd
ol, dt, and dd
ul, dt, and li
questions.explanation: A description list uses dl as the wrapper, dt for the term, and dd for the description. li is used only in ul and ol.

questions.q: How do you create a nested list?
options: Place a new ul or ol directly inside the parent ul or ol
Place a new ul or ol inside an li element
Use the nested attribute on the parent list
Create a second list after the first and indent it with CSS
questions.explanation: The nested list goes inside an li element, not directly inside the parent ul or ol. That is valid HTML — placing it directly in ul or ol is not.

questions.prompt: Complete this ordered list of steps.
questions.code:
<___>
  <___>Boil the water</___>
  <___>Add the pasta</___>
  <___>Cook for 10 minutes</___>
</___>
questions.explanation: ol wraps an ordered list. Each item is wrapped in li. The closing tags match their opening tags.

questions.code:
<___>
  <___>viewport</___>
  <___>The visible area of a web page in the browser window</___>
</___>
questions.explanation: dl wraps the description list. dt holds the term. dd holds the definition.

lessons.title: Links and the Anchor Element
lessons.content:
Links are the foundation of the web. The a element, short for anchor, creates a hyperlink. The href attribute holds the destination URL.

URLs can be absolute or relative. An absolute URL includes the full address including the protocol: https://example.com/page. A relative URL is a path relative to the current file: /about or ../images/photo.jpg.

The target attribute controls where the link opens. target='_blank' opens the link in a new tab. When you use target='_blank', you should also add rel='noopener noreferrer' for security reasons — without it, the opened page can access and manipulate the page that opened it.

Links can wrap more than just text. You can wrap an image in an a element to make the image itself a clickable link.

Fragment links scroll to a specific part of the same page. Set href to a hash followed by an id: href='#section-two'. The target element must have that id attribute set.

examples.label: Link variations
examples.code: External link Relative link Opens in new tab Jump to contact section

questions.q: What attribute on an anchor element defines where the link goes?
options: src, the same as an image
href, which holds the destination URL
url, which is the link attribute
link, which is the standard attribute name
questions.explanation: href stands for hypertext reference. It holds the URL the link points to. src is used on media elements like img and script, not on anchors.

questions.q: Why should rel='noopener noreferrer' be added alongside target='_blank'?
options: It makes the link open faster in the new tab
It prevents the new tab from accessing the original page, closing a security vulnerability
It is required for the link to open in a new tab at all
It hides the referrer information from analytics tools
questions.explanation: Without noopener, a page opened with target='_blank' can use window.opener to access and manipulate the original tab. noopener closes that vector. noreferrer also suppresses referrer header information.

questions.q: You want a link that jumps to a section with id='pricing' on the same page. What is the correct href value?
options: href='pricing'
href='id:pricing'
href='#pricing'
href='@pricing'
questions.explanation: Fragment links use a hash followed by the target element's id value. The browser scrolls that element into view without loading a new page.

lessons.title: Images
lessons.content:
The img element embeds an image. It is a void element — no closing tag. The two required attributes are src, which is the path or URL of the image file, and alt, which is a text description.

The alt attribute is not optional. If the image fails to load, the alt text is shown instead. Screen readers read it aloud to users who cannot see the image. Search engines use it to understand image content. If an image is purely decorative and adds no information, use an empty alt attribute: alt=''. This tells screen readers to skip it. But never leave alt out entirely.

width and height attributes should be set on images. They tell the browser the image dimensions before it loads so it can reserve the correct space in the layout. This prevents layout shifts as the page loads.

The figure and figcaption elements wrap an image and its caption. figure is a semantic container for self-contained content. figcaption is the visible caption text below the image.

examples.label: Images with attributes and figure

questions.q: A purely decorative image that adds no information should have which alt value?
options: No alt attribute at all
alt='decorative'
alt='image'
alt='' — an empty string
questions.explanation: An empty alt tells screen readers to skip the image entirely. Omitting alt is different — it signals that the author forgot it, not that the image is decorative. alt='decorative' or alt='image' is meaningless filler.

questions.q: Why should you set width and height attributes on images?
options: To prevent the image from being downloaded at full resolution
So the browser can reserve space before the image loads and avoid layout shifts
To enable zoom and pan gestures on the image
Because browsers cannot display images without explicit dimensions
questions.explanation: When the browser knows dimensions upfront, it allocates the right amount of space in the layout before the file arrives. Without them, the page jumps as images load in, which is a poor user experience.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the figure element?
options: It applies a border and shadow to images automatically
It is a semantic container for self-contained content like images with captions
It replaces the img element in modern HTML
It makes images responsive across screen sizes
questions.explanation: figure wraps content that is referenced from the main flow but could stand on its own, like an image with a caption. figcaption goes inside it as the visible description.

lessons.title: Audio and Video
lessons.content:
HTML5 introduced native audio and video elements. You no longer need Flash or plugins to embed media.

The video element embeds a video. The src attribute points to the video file. The controls attribute adds the browser's built-in playback controls. Without controls, the video has no play button and the user cannot interact with it. autoplay starts the video automatically when the page loads — use this carefully and combine it with muted, because browsers block autoplay with sound by default. loop repeats the video when it ends.

The audio element works the same way. It also uses src and controls.

Both elements support multiple source files using the source element inside them. This lets you provide the same media in different formats so the browser picks one it supports. The type attribute on source tells the browser the file format.

The poster attribute on video specifies an image to display before the video plays, like a thumbnail.

examples.label: Video and audio with fallback sources
examples.code:
<video controls width='640' height='360' poster='thumbnail.jpg'>
  <source src='video.mp4' type='video/mp4'>
  <source src='video.webm' type='video/webm'>
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>

<audio controls>
  <source src='audio.mp3' type='audio/mpeg'>
  <source src='audio.ogg' type='audio/ogg'>
  Your browser does not support audio.
</audio>

questions.q: What does the controls attribute do on a video element?
options: It enables keyboard shortcuts for the video
It adds the browser's built-in play, pause, and seek interface
It prevents the user from pausing the video
It enables fullscreen mode only
questions.explanation: Without controls, there is no visible interface on the video at all. The controls attribute adds the browser's native playback UI — play, pause, volume, seek, and fullscreen.

questions.q: Why provide multiple source elements inside a video element?
options: So the video plays from multiple servers simultaneously for speed
Because HTML5 video requires at least two formats to function
Because different browsers support different video formats, so you offer options
To increase video quality on high-resolution screens
questions.explanation: Browser support for video formats varies. By providing mp4 and webm, you cover the major browsers. The browser picks the first format it can play and ignores the rest.

questions.q: What does the poster attribute on a video element do?
options: Adds a caption below the video player
Sets an image to display as the video thumbnail before playback starts
Loads a preview clip of the first few seconds
Overlays a watermark on the video during playback
questions.explanation: poster lets you specify an image that shows in the video area before the user hits play. It gives the player a polished look instead of showing a blank frame.

lessons.title: Spot the Bug: Inaccessible Video
lessons.prompt: The video on this page will load but the user cannot interact with it. Click the line causing the problem.
lessons.code:
Watch the Tutorial

Follow along with the steps below.
lessons.explanation: The video element is missing the controls attribute. Without it, there is no play button, no seek bar, and no way for the user to interact with the video. Add controls to give users a playback interface.
